blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e. $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relationship:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This relationship shows how the total differential $dz$ is computed using partial derivatives with respect to $x$ and $y$.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of a function and change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$, $y$, and $z$.

blocks.description: The figure illustrates a point on a surface represented by $z$ versus $x$ and $y$. It conveys the idea of how changes in $x$ and $y$ lead to a change in $z$ as the point moves on the surface.

blocks.latex: \Delta z = \text{change in } z \text{ due to the change in } x + \text{ change in } z \text{ due to the change in } y.
blocks.description: This expresses how the total change in $z$ can be attributed to changes in both $x$ and $y$.

blocks.text: Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface. The change of position is made by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21). The change in $z$ due to the change in position is important because it shows how $ rac{dz}{dx}$ and $ rac{dz}{dy}$ affect the total change.
